content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

November 18, 2009

Norbert J. Bartosik
General Manager/CEO
California Exposition & State Fair
P.O. Box 15649
Sacramento, California 95852-1649

RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-09-182

Dear Mr. Bartosik:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the gift provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ This letter is based on the facts presented; the Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Because you seek general information, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.²

QUESTION

What, if any, of the state fair credentials provided to California State Fair Board Members are considered gifts under the Act?

CONCLUSION

The state fair credentials provided to California State Fair Board members as outlined herein are gifts to those members if they provide a personal benefit to the member, to the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received, unless there is an exemption provided, the items are claimed as income, or the agency follows the procedures outlined in Regulation 18944.1.

_______________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed).

File No.I-09-182
Page No. 2

FACTS

You are the General Manager/CEO for the California Exposition and State Fair (the
"State Fair") and seek advice regarding the gift provisions of the Act with respect to the "State
Fair Credentials" provided to the members of the State Fair board of directors. In a memo to the
board of directors dated August 18, 2009, prior to the beginning of this year's fair, you stated:

"Board Members are provided with State Fair Credentials for personal and
courtesy usage and to distribute at their discretion for the public purpose of
promoting the annual California State Fair. Per the Fair Political Practices
Commission limitations, which were reviewed by the Executive Committee in
2009, the value of credentials that Board Members can receive without violating
the gift limit is $420. The value of the credentials you are being issued, (is $414;
and broken out as noted below); and, does not exceed this limit. You will be
required to report that amount in the next reporting cycle, with our assistance.
Enclosed are the following State Fair Credentials for your personal use during the
2009 State Fair.

Among the items provided are:

• Ten State Fair admission tickets valued at a pre-fair "Poppy Pak" price of $7 apiece
($70).
• Eight Lot C parking Passes with a pre-fair value of $7 apiece ($56).
• Script for food and beverage in lieu of any claim for per diem food reimbursement (no
reportable value).
• One Director's Card good for parking and fair admission for the cardholder and his or her
party (no reportable value).
• Four VIP guest cards. These are season pass admissions valued at $72 and good for all
days of the fair for the bearer and one guest. They are to be used for "public relations"
purposes ($288).

The State Fair also includes thoroughbred racing and a concert series, with different
artists performing each night of the fair. Admission to these events is free with admission to the
State Fair. You would like to know if the policy outlined above complies with the Act
requirements regarding gift.

ANALYSIS

The Act establishes a statutory and regulatory scheme to reduce influences on public
officials' from the receipt of gifts. First, it prohibits certain public officials from accepting large
gifts (currently defined as more than $420) from an identified source. (Section 89503, Regulation
18940.2.) Second, it requires certain public officials to disclose their receipt of any gifts

³ Section defines "public official" as every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local
government agency. California State Fair Board Members are public officials.

File No.I-09-182
Page No. 3

aggregating to $50 or more from identified sources, so that the public is made aware of such
gifts. (Sections 87207 and 87302.) Finally, it prohibits a public official from using his or her
position to influence the outcome of a decision involving the donor of a gift valued at $420 or
more. (Section 87100, 87103(e), Regulation 18940.2.)

Section 82028 defines gift as:

"any payment that confers a personal benefit on the recipient, to the extent that
consideration of equal or greater value is not received and includes a rebate or
discount in the price of anything of value unless the rebate or discount is made in
the regular course of business to members of the public without regard to official
status."

Public officials who are covered by the Act's gift disclosure provisions are required to
disclose gifts from certain specified sources either pursuant to Section 87200 or pursuant to the
terms of their agency's conflict-of-interest code, formulated in accordance with the Act's
provisions.

Recently, the Commission adopted amendments to Regulation 18944.1 (copy enclosed)
concerning tickets to entertainment events. This regulation sets up a procedure for agencies that
provide tickets to their officials that, if utilized, will establish that the official receiving the ticket
has met the burden under Section 82028 that equal or greater value has been provided in
exchange therefor. Accordingly, under this procedure, no gift will have been received by the
official.⁴ An agency is free to make its own choice whether or not to adopt a policy conforming
to the regulation or to treat the tickets as income or gifts to the official and not apply the
regulation. (Givner Advice Letter, I-09-223.)

Your memo indicates that the State Fair credentials are being treated as gifts rather than
income, and your agency does not appear to have adopted a policy in conformance with
Regulation 18944.1. Your question therefore, appears to be limited to whether or not the
reported values indicated comply with the provisions of the Act. Accordingly, our analysis is
limited to the values of the items listed above.

Fair Admission and Parking. You have indicated that each director is provided 10 State
Fair admission tickets and parking passes for Lot C. The State Fair offers tickets to the general
public at a pre-fair price of $70 for a ten pack ($7 per ticket). Parking passes are similarly sold at
a per-fair price of $7 apiece, so the eight passes provided are valued at $56. Regulation 18946.1
provides that tickets or passes are "valued at the face value of the pass or ticket, provided that the
face value is a price that was, or otherwise would have been, offered to the general public.
Therefore, the values you have indicated conform to the regulation.

Script for Food and Beverages. You have indicated that the script for food and beverages
is provided to the board members in lieu of per diem food reimbursement. So long as it is used

⁴ The regulation also states that the ticket may, in the alternative, be treated as income, in which case the
regulation's provisions will not apply.

File No.I-09-182
Page No. 4

for per diem reimbursement in accordance with the responsibilities of the position, the script for
food and beverages for the board member's personal use only is not a gift (or income) and need
not be reported (see Regulation 82030(b)(2)).

Director's Card. This card provides free parking and admission to the fair for the
director and his or her "party." You have indicated it carries no reportable value. It appears the
word "party" refers to all those "in your vehicle" upon entry. We recognize that director's
receive free admission to the fair in conjunction with performing their responsibilities as
directors and that this may even include being accompanied by ones immediate family.
However, the free admission benefit would not extend to anyone other than the director and his
or her immediate family. The use of the admission for anyone not an immediate family member
is considered a gift to the director from the agency.

VIP Guest Cards. These four season passes are to be used by the director to distribute to
others. From the information provided, it appears that these passes may qualify under the
provisions of Regulation 18944.1, if the agency were to adopt a policy thereunder. Absent that,
the season passes would need to be reported as either gifts or income received by the director.

Finally, you have indicated that admission to the fair includes free admission to other
events, such as horse racing and concerts. Because these events are free with admission, they
have no separate value and need not be reported. If these events required a separate ticket, the
value that would need to be reported would be determined by the face value rule stated in
Regulation 18946.1.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By: William J. Lenkeit
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

WJL:jgl

Enclosure

written advice. (Section 831 l4; regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed).
